sandbox_vpc.isolation_rules: No VPC peering, no Transit Gateway, no Direct Connect. NAT Gateway omitted — EC2 instance uses IGW directly for inbound RDP simulation. All inbound controlled by security group. VPC flow logs enabled on ALL traffic.

resources.name: dangerdev-log-bucket
resources.purpose: Central sink for CloudTrail logs, VPC flow logs, GuardDuty finding exports, and S3 access logs. Must exist before all other resources that write logs.
resources.configuration_notes: Bucket name: dangerdev-lab-logs-{account_id}. Block all public access. Bucket policy: allow cloudtrail.amazonaws.com and guardduty.amazonaws.com service principals to PutObject under their respective prefixes. SSE: aws:kms. Lifecycle: expire after 90 days. Enable MFA delete.

resources.name: dangerdev-sandbox-vpc
resources.purpose: Isolated VPC for all EC2 infrastructure. Provides network boundary and flow log telemetry for T1021.001 RDP detection.
resources.configuration_notes: CIDR: 10.99.0.0/16. enable_dns_hostnames: true. enable_dns_support: true. Attach VPC flow log (ALL traffic) to IAM role with s3:PutObject on dangerdev-log-bucket/flow-logs/. Do NOT peer or attach Transit Gateway.
resources.cleanup_method: pulumi destroy — must run after all dependent EC2 resources are terminated

resources.name: dangerdev-internet-gateway
resources.purpose: Internet Gateway enabling inbound RDP to the attack-surface EC2 instance and simulated outbound mining pool traffic.
resources.configuration_notes: Attach to dangerdev-sandbox-vpc. Create a route table with 0.0.0.0/0 → igw and associate it with dangerdev-public-subnet.

resources.name: dangerdev-public-subnet
resources.purpose: Public subnet hosting the RDP-exposed EC2 instance.
resources.configuration_notes: CIDR: 10.99.1.0/24. AZ: us-east-1a. map_public_ip_on_launch: true. Associate with the IGW route table created in dangerdev-internet-gateway.

resources.name: dangerdev-exposed-admin-user
resources.purpose: Pre-existing victim IAM user with AdministratorAccess whose long-term access key is leaked into the bait tfstate object. This is the T1078.004 initial access vector — the accidentally exposed credential.
resources.configuration_notes: Username: lab-infra-admin. Attach arn:aws:iam::aws:policy/AdministratorAccess. Create one long-term access key via aws_iam_access_key resource; export key_id and secret into the dangerdev-leaked-creds-bucket tfstate object during provisioning (see bait bucket). Do NOT enable MFA — replicates the real exposure. No password / console login needed; key-only access.

resources.name: dangerdev-decoy-user-alice
resources.purpose: Realistic IAM user visible in ListUsers (T1087.004 discovery) and targeted for access key creation and password reset during T1098 account manipulation emulation.
resources.configuration_notes: Username: alice.chen. Attach arn:aws:iam::aws:policy/AmazonS3ReadOnlyAccess. Create console login profile with password 'ChangeMe2024!'. Create one existing access key (the emulation will add a second key via iam:CreateAccessKey and reset the password via iam:UpdateLoginProfile). This user is the manipulation target.

resources.name: dangerdev-decoy-user-ops
resources.purpose: Service-account-style IAM user populating the ListUsers response to inform realistic masquerade naming (T1036.005). The 'ses' masquerade mimics auto-generated SES SMTP service account patterns.
resources.configuration_notes: Username: ops-automation. Attach arn:aws:iam::aws:policy/AmazonEC2ReadOnlyAccess. No console login. Create one access key (key is not used — exists to simulate a real service account). Also create a second decoy user named ses-smtp-user.20231105-091212 (mimics the SES auto-generated SMTP credential username format that DangerDev's 'ses' username was designed to blend into).

resources.name: dangerdev-honey-iam-user
resources.purpose: Canary IAM user whose credentials, if used, trigger an immediate CloudWatch alert — detects credential replay. Appears in ListUsers output as a realistic service account.
resources.configuration_notes: Username: backup-restore-svc. Create access key and store securely. Configure EventBridge rule: source=aws.iam OR any CloudTrail event where userIdentity.userName=backup-restore-svc → SNS topic alert. The access key itself is never distributed; any API call using it indicates unauthorized replay.
resources.cleanup_method: iam:DeleteAccessKey; iam:DeleteUser; remove EventBridge rule and SNS topic

resources.name: dangerdev-leaked-creds-bucket
resources.purpose: S3 bucket containing a terraform.tfstate object with plaintext IAM credentials embedded, simulating the external storage exposure that enables T1078.004 initial access.
resources.configuration_notes: Bucket name: dangerdev-infra-state-{account_id}. Block all public access (no ACL, no bucket policy granting public). Object key: infra/prod/terraform.tfstate — a realistic Terraform state JSON containing the access_key_id and secret_access_key from dangerdev-exposed-admin-user embedded under a resource block (e.g., aws_iam_access_key.lab_admin). Enable S3 server access logging to dangerdev-log-bucket/s3-access/. Versioning: enabled.

resources.name: dangerdev-sensitive-s3-bucket
resources.purpose: Bucket with realistic-looking objects discovered via s3:ListBuckets during T1530 collection phase. Provides a credible discovery target without real PII.
resources.configuration_notes: Bucket name: dangerdev-prod-data-archive-{account_id}. Block all public access. SSE: AES256. Versioning: enabled. S3 access logging to dangerdev-log-bucket. Objects (empty files with realistic names): customers/export_2024_q4.csv, backups/db_dump_prod.sql.gz, reports/financial_summary_2024.xlsx. Enable CloudTrail S3 data events (GetObject, PutObject) on this bucket specifically.

resources.name: dangerdev-secretsmanager-secret
resources.purpose: Secrets Manager secret probed indirectly via iam:SimulatePrincipalPolicy during T1518.001 — TA checks permissions without triggering actual GetSecretValue calls on the service.
resources.configuration_notes: Secret name: prod/database/master_credentials. Value (fake): {"username":"admin","password":"Sup3rS3cr3t!","host":"prod-mysql.us-east-1.rds.amazonaws.com","port":3306}. Resource policy: deny GetSecretValue from any principal except lab operator role (to make SimulatePrincipalPolicy return 'implicitDeny' — exactly what TA was checking). Rotation: disabled. This value is entirely fabricated and contains no real credentials.

resources.name: dangerdev-ses-email-identity
resources.purpose: Verified SES email identity enabling T1526 discovery calls (GetSendQuota, ListIdentities) and logging T1566.002 SendEmail API calls in CloudTrail — SES sandbox mode blocks all external delivery.
resources.configuration_notes: Verify a lab-controlled domain only (e.g., emulation-lab-noreply.example.com via Route53 hosted zone). Keep account in SES sandbox — do NOT submit a sending limit increase request or request production access. This ensures ses:SendEmail calls are fully logged in CloudTrail but zero external delivery occurs. Configure DKIM signing for realism. Do NOT use PayPal-mimicking or any third-party brand domains.

resources.name: dangerdev-open-sg
resources.purpose: Security group with RDP (3389) and SSH (22) open to 0.0.0.0/0, replicating the wide-open group DangerDev created for EC2 access. Intentionally violates Security Hub EC2.19 to generate findings.
resources.configuration_notes: VPC: dangerdev-sandbox-vpc. Ingress: TCP 3389 from 0.0.0.0/0; TCP 22 from 0.0.0.0/0. Egress: all traffic (0.0.0.0/0) — required for simulated outbound mining pool traffic observable in flow logs. Name tag: dangerdev-open-sg so it is obvious this is a lab artifact.

resources.name: dangerdev-ec2-keypair
resources.purpose: EC2 key pair created as part of the T1578.002 instance setup sequence. Required to RDP into the Windows instance for T1021.001 simulation.
resources.configuration_notes: Key pair name: dangerdev-lab-key. Generate externally and import public key, or use aws_key_pair with generated RSA key. Store .pem locally in lab secrets vault — do NOT commit to source control or upload to S3.

resources.name: dangerdev-ssm-instance-role
resources.purpose: Minimal IAM role for the EC2 instance profile — grants SSM Session Manager access for lab operator management only. Deliberately narrow to prevent host-plane privilege escalation during emulation.
resources.configuration_notes: Assume role policy: ec2.amazonaws.com. Attach arn:aws:iam::aws:policy/AmazonSSMManagedInstanceCore only. This allows lab operators to shell into the instance via SSM without requiring the 0.0.0.0/0 SSH rule to be open to their own IPs (that rule is the attack surface, not the access method).

resources.name: dangerdev-ec2-instance-profile
resources.purpose: Instance profile wrapping dangerdev-ssm-instance-role for attachment to the EC2 Windows instance.
resources.configuration_notes: Associate with dangerdev-ssm-instance-role. Attach to dangerdev-ec2-windows-instance via iam_instance_profile argument.

resources.name: dangerdev-ec2-windows-instance
resources.purpose: EC2 Windows t2.micro with port 3389 open to 0.0.0.0/0, serving as the RDP attack surface for T1021.001 and the lifecycle anchor for T1578.002. Substitutes for p3.16xlarge per T1496 documented-only constraint.
resources.configuration_notes: AMI: latest Windows Server 2022 Base (us-east-1). Instance type: t2.micro — NOT p3.16xlarge (GPU mining is documented-only per cannot_safely_emulate). subnet_id: dangerdev-public-subnet. security_groups: [dangerdev-open-sg]. key_name: dangerdev-lab-key. iam_instance_profile: dangerdev-ec2-instance-profile. metadata_options: http_tokens=optional (IMDSv1 — mirrors DangerDev's default VPC behavior). associate_public_ip_address: true. user_data: see userdata_actions.
resources.cleanup_method: ec2:TerminateInstances — wait for terminated state before destroying security group

resources.name: dangerdev-cross-account-backdoor-role
resources.purpose: IAM role named AWSeservedSSO_AdminAccess (lowercase 'e' masquerade of AWSReservedSSO_*) with cross-account trust, simulating T1199 trusted relationship backdoor and T1036.005 masquerading.
resources.configuration_notes: Role name: AWSeservedSSO_AdminAccess. Trust policy principal: arn:aws:iam::ADVERSARY_LAB_ACCOUNT_ID:root — replace ADVERSARY_LAB_ACCOUNT_ID with the ID of a separately provisioned isolated adversary AWS account used only for this emulation. Attach arn:aws:iam::aws:policy/AdministratorAccess. The one-character difference (lowercase 'e' vs uppercase 'R') from AWSReservedSSO_* is the masquerade per T1036.005. NOTE: AssumeRole execution requires the adversary lab account to be separately configured and credentialed.

resources.name: dangerdev-configrecorder-backdoor-role
resources.purpose: Second IAM backdoor role named AWSLanding-Zones-ConfigRecorderRoles masquerading as a legitimate AWS Config service role, used for T1036.005 masquerading and T1199 cross-account persistence.
resources.configuration_notes: Role name: AWSLanding-Zones-ConfigRecorderRoles. Trust policy principal: arn:aws:iam::ADVERSARY_LAB_ACCOUNT_ID:root (same isolated adversary account as dangerdev-cross-account-backdoor-role). Attach arn:aws:iam::aws:policy/AdministratorAccess. This role mimics legitimate AWS Config Recorder roles that exist in AWS Organizations landing zones — chosen by DangerDev specifically because it blends into enterprise AWS environments.

resources.name: dangerdev-guardduty-detector
resources.purpose: GuardDuty detector generating findings for all emulated techniques. Required as a target for T1518.001 where DangerDev reviews ListFindings/GetFindings to assess their detection exposure before cleanup.
resources.configuration_notes: enable: true. finding_publishing_frequency: FIFTEEN_MINUTES during active emulation runs (change to SIX_HOURS at rest to reduce cost). Enable S3 protection. Do NOT configure suppression rules — all findings must remain visible for detection validation. Configure finding export to dangerdev-log-bucket/guardduty-findings/ via aws.guardduty.PublishingDestination.

resources.name: dangerdev-cloudtrail
resources.purpose: Multi-region CloudTrail capturing all management and data events for every emulated technique. Primary audit source for detection validation across all 15 emulated techniques.
resources.configuration_notes: Trail name: dangerdev-emulation-trail. is_multi_region_trail: true. include_global_service_events: true (required to capture IAM events which are global). s3_bucket_name: dangerdev-log-bucket, prefix: cloudtrail/. enable_log_file_validation: true. CloudWatch Logs integration: create log group /aws/cloudtrail/dangerdev and IAM role allowing cloudtrail.amazonaws.com to CreateLogStream and PutLogEvents. Data events: enable s3:GetObject and s3:PutObject on both dangerdev-sensitive-s3-bucket and dangerdev-leaked-creds-bucket. Management events: ALL (Read + Write).

userdata_actions.description: Enable RDP at the Windows OS level to match the security group exposure. Without this, the open security group port would be blocked by Windows Firewall, preventing T1021.001 simulation.
commands: netsh advfirewall firewall set rule group='remote desktop' new enable=yes

userdata_actions.description: Simulate GPU cryptomining lifecycle using a benign CPU-bound workload on t2.micro. Runs for 5 minutes then exits — produces a CPU spike observable in CloudWatch metrics and generates VPC flow log outbound traffic patterns. Does NOT install or run any real mining software. GPU instances (p3.16xlarge) are excluded per cannot_safely_emulate constraint.
commands: Write-EventLog -LogName Application -Source 'Application' -EventId 9999 -Message 'T1496-simulation: benign CPU workload complete'

iam_policies.name: dangerdev-exposed-admin-user-policy
iam_policies.reason_over_permissioned: Intentional: replicates T1078.004 — victim account had AdministratorAccess attached to the user whose long-term key was accidentally exposed. This is the exact policy attachment that made the credential leak catastrophic.

iam_policies.name: dangerdev-cross-account-backdoor-role-policy
iam_policies.reason_over_permissioned: Intentional: replicates T1199 cross-account persistence. DangerDev attached AdministratorAccess to the backdoor role so the external account could assume full control even after the original IAM user was deleted. Backdoor survives user deletion — no user artifact remains in the victim account.

iam_policies.name: dangerdev-configrecorder-backdoor-role-policy
iam_policies.reason_over_permissioned: Intentional: replicates second T1199 backdoor. Named to impersonate an AWS Config service role (legitimate AWS Config roles carry AWSConfigRole, not AdministratorAccess) — the policy mismatch is itself a detection opportunity for T1036.005.
